***** After the hearing, the Court signs an order denying defendants' motion to strike punitive damages claims and portions of the First Amened Complaint. (See signed order for complete ruling.) (302/RBU) *****

Jones Notice Motion Amend And Memo Ps And As
Off Calendar Apr-20-2021 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
PLAINTIFF TAMREN JONES' Motion for Leave to Amend to File First Amended Complaint.

Plaintiff's motion for leave to file first amended complaint is granted. The City concedes that the motion is timely and that granting the motion will not prejudice it. The Court cannot conclude as a matter of law that the proposed amendment would be futile. The City's opposition raises factual issues, such as whether (as Plaintiff alleges) defendant Truong intentionally ran a red light. If the City contends that the allegations in the proposed first amended complaint are insufficient to support a prayer for punitive damages or are otherwise inappropriate, it may file a motion to strike; alternatively, it may file a motion for summary adjudication or other appropriate motion. (See, e.g., California Casualty Gen. Ins. v. Superior Court (1985) 173 Cal.App.3d 274, 281 ["the preferable practice would be to permit the amendment and allow the parties to test its legal sufficiency by demurrer, motion for judgment on the pleadings or other appropriate proceedings."].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda; Not Reported. (302/EPS)

Defendant Vinh Quock Notice Of Motion And Motion To Enforce Stipulation And Enter Judgment In Favor Of Defendant Quock C.C.P. 664.6
Off Calendar Sep-16-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO and VINH TRUONG's Motion To Enforce Stipulation And Enter Judgment In Favor Of Defendant Quock C.C.P. 664.6.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Vinh Quock's motion to enforce stipulation and enter judgment in Quock's favor is denied. On or about February 28, 2020, counsel to the parties entered into a written stipulation by which the City and County of San Francisco agreed that in exchange for a dismissal without prejudice of Quock, a Muni bus driver, the City would produce him in for deposition and he would agree to appear at trial in compliance with the requirements of the Code of Civil Procedure for appearance of a party witness. (Kirby Decl., Ex. C.) However, on April 21, 2020, plaintiff's counsel purported to "withdraw" his agreement to that stipulation. (Id., Ex. E.) The stipulation was not "a writing signed by the parties" within the meaning of Code of Civil Procedure section 664.6. The term "parties" as used in that statute "means the litigants themselves, and does not include their attorneys of record." (Levy v. Superior Court (1995) 10 Cal.4th 578, 586; accord, Davidson v. Superior Court (1999) 70 Cal.App.4th 514, 528 [settlement agreement held unenforceable because based on stipulated settlement signed only by counsel]; Williams v. Saunders (1997) 55 Cal.App.4th 1158, 1162-1163 [signatures of defendant's attorney and codefendant spouse did not bind defendant to settlement agreement]; Robertson v. Chen (1996) 44 Cal.App.4th 1290, 1292-1294 [trial court erred in granting plaintiffs' motion for enforcement of mandatory settlement conference statement signed only by counsel for one side, but not by the parties]; see also Mesa RHF Partners, L.P. v. City of Los Angeles (2019) 33 Cal.App.5th 913, 918 [neither requests for dismissal signed only by counsel nor settlement agreements which were not transmitted to the trial court before the cases were dismissed preserved court's jurisdiction to enforce settlement agreement].) In any event, the relief defendant seeks is pointless, since plaintiff could simply rename him as a party after a dismissal without prejudice.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/EPS)
